headline: GRAINS STEADY AS PLANTING WINDOW OPENS
subheadline: Markets mark time ahead of crucial weather-driven weeks.
lead: Corn held $4.43 while soybeans barely budged at $11.68 as traders await the real action: planting season weather patterns. With optimal corn planting starting Monday across the Corn Belt, every forecast becomes market-moving. Chicago wheat fell a penny to $5.71 on global competition concerns.
teaser: Calm before the planting storm — weather about to take the wheel.

one_number.context: December corn at $4.73 shows the market pricing decent production assumptions. That 30-cent carry over nearby reflects normal storage economics, but one week of planting delays could collapse that spread quickly. The December contract is your benchmark for new-crop pricing decisions.

sections.title: Grains & Oilseeds
sections.body: The calm won't last. Corn at $4.43 and soybeans at $11.68 reflect a market that's positioned for normal planting progress starting Monday. Weather models become the primary price driver from here through May 15. December corn at $4.73 suggests the market isn't pricing significant planting delays yet. Wheat's penny drop to $5.71 reflects ongoing export struggles against aggressive Black Sea competition.
sections.bottom_line: Steady prices mask building weather volatility potential.
sections.farmer_action: Monitor 7-14 day forecasts religiously — pricing windows will open and close rapidly based on weather.

sections.title: Livestock & Dairy
sections.body: Live cattle pushed higher to $247.53 on continued strong margins and tight supplies. Feedlots remain cautious on placements with feed costs still elevated. Lean hogs eased to $104.17 after recent volatility, but processors are paying up for available supplies. Class III milk gained slightly to $17.06 as spring flush approaches peak production.
sections.bottom_line: Cattle strength continues, hogs consolidating recent gains.
sections.farmer_action: Cattle producers should price remaining unhedged inventory — these historically strong levels deserve protection.

sections.title: Energy & Inputs
sections.body: Crude oil barely moved at $97.88 while natural gas dropped to $2.65 on continued mild weather. Diesel demand spikes are coming as tractors start rolling next week. Fertilizer prices remain elevated but stable, with anhydrous ammonia supply chains functioning normally. Planting season historically drives 15-20% diesel demand increases across the Corn Belt.
sections.bottom_line: Energy steady now, but planting demand surge approaches.
sections.farmer_action: Lock remaining diesel needs immediately — current levels are manageable before seasonal demand spike.

sections.title: Macro & Trade
sections.body: The S&P 500 gained to $6,824 continuing its recent strength. Dollar index steady at $100.00 keeps export competitiveness neutral. Trade flows remain normal but Chinese soybean buying patterns suggest they're comfortable with current price levels. Gold held $4,789 as inflation concerns persist amid strong economic data.
sections.bottom_line: Stable macro backdrop supports agricultural fundamentals.
sections.farmer_action: Focus on production decisions over macro hedging — fundamentals driving the bus right now.

the_more_you_know.title: Why Corn Planting Dates Actually Matter
the_more_you_know.body: Every day you plant corn after April 20th in Wisconsin costs you roughly 1 bushel per acre in yield potential. That's not marketing hype — it's 30 years of university data. By May 15th, you're looking at 6-8 bushel penalties on average. The yield loss accelerates because later planting pushes pollination into July heat stress windows and grain fill into frost risk periods. This is why weather becomes the only thing that matters for the next month.

watch_list.desc: 7-day weather models for Corn Belt — any wet pattern changes everything
First weekly planting progress report sets baseline expectations
China soybean tender announcements — they've been quiet lately

daily_quote.text: Don't tell me how hard you work. Show me your field in August.

source_summary: CBOT futures, USDA reports, weather models
